puzzles.title: Select All
puzzles.prompt: Return all rows from the table 'users'.
puzzles.hint: users table has id, name, age, score, email columns
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, age INTEGER, score INTEGER, email TEXT);
puzzles.narrative: Everything is here. All the rows. Nothing is missing yet.
hints: SELECT retrieves columns from a table
* is a wildcard that means all columns
SELECT * FROM users

puzzles.title: Filter by Age
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, age INTEGER, score INTEGER, email TEXT);
puzzles.narrative: Some rows didn't come back. WHERE did they go. WHERE.
hints: WHERE filters rows based on a condition
comparison operators: =, >, <, >=, <=
WHERE age > 30

puzzles.title: Count Rows
puzzles.prompt: Count how many users exist in the table.
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, age INTEGER, score INTEGER, email TEXT);
puzzles.narrative: How many are left. Count carefully. The number keeps changing.
hints: aggregate functions collapse multiple rows into one
COUNT() counts rows
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users

puzzles.title: Order by Score
puzzles.prompt: Select all players ordered by score descending.
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE players (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, score INTEGER);
puzzles.narrative: The order matters. It always mattered. You just didn't notice until now.
hints: ORDER BY sorts the result set
ASC is ascending, DESC is descending
ORDER BY score DESC

puzzles.title: Join Tables
puzzles.prompt: Return all orders with their associated user names.
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, age INTEGER, score INTEGER, email TEXT); CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, user_id INTEGER, product TEXT);
puzzles.seed_data: INSERT INTO users VALUES (1,'Alice',25,88,'alice@mail.com'),(2,'Bob',34,72,'bob@mail.com'),(3,'Carol',29,95,'carol@mail.com'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (1,1,'Widget'),(2,2,'Gadget'),(3,1,'Doohickey'),(4,3,'Thingamajig');
puzzles.narrative: Two tables. One truth. The join is the only thing holding them together.
hints: JOIN combines rows from two tables based on a condition
ON specifies how the tables relate
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id

puzzles.title: Group and Aggregate
puzzles.prompt: Return each department and the number of employees in it.
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INTEGER);
puzzles.narrative: Groups form. Some are larger than others. One stands alone.
hints: GROUP BY collapses rows with the same value into one
COUNT(*) counts rows per group
SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department

puzzles.title: Find Duplicates
puzzles.prompt: Find all emails that appear more than once in the users table.
puzzles.starter_code:
SELECT email, COUNT(*)
FROM users
GROUP BY email
HAVING -- TODO;
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, age INTEGER, score INTEGER, email TEXT);
puzzles.narrative: Some things appear twice. They were never supposed to. Neither were you.
hints: HAVING filters groups after aggregation
it is like WHERE but for grouped results
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1

puzzles.title: Subquery Challenge
puzzles.prompt: Select all users whose score is above the average score.
puzzles.hint: use a subquery to calculate the average
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, age INTEGER, score INTEGER, email TEXT);
puzzles.narrative: Above average. That used to mean something. The average keeps dropping.
hints: a subquery is a SELECT inside another SELECT
AVG() calculates the average of a column
WHERE score > (SELECT AVG(score) FROM users)

puzzles.title: Window Function Wizardry
puzzles.prompt: For each employee return their name, salary, and rank based on salary highest first.
puzzles.schema: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INTEGER);
puzzles.narrative: Everyone is ranked. Everyone has always been ranked. Now you can see it.
hints: window functions operate across rows without collapsing them
RANK() assigns a rank within an ordered partition
RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC)
